en:What is the exact CBAM certificate fo: What is the exact CBAM certificate formula?
en:Per Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and its: Per Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and its implementing acts: (1) CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (tonnes) × Embedded Emission Factor (tCO₂e/tonne) × CBAM Phase-in Factor. (2) Certificate cost (€) = CBAM Certificates × ETS weekly average auction price (€/tCO₂e). The embedded emission factor is either your verified actual installation data or the Commission's country-specific default values per Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621. The CBAM phase-in factor reflects the phase-out of ETS free allowances: 2.5% in 2026, 5% in 2027, 10% in 2028, rising to 100% in 2034.
My EU customer needs emissions data. What exactly must I provide?
en:Your EU importer needs installation-l: Your EU importer needs installation-level embedded emissions data in the EU Communication Template format. This includes: total embedded direct emissions per tonne of each CBAM product produced at your installation, indirect electricity emissions where required (cement, fertilisers), the production route classification, and evidence of any eligible carbon price paid in India (if applicable). Data must cover the full calendar year of imports (e.g., full 2026 for the September 2027 declaration). All data must be calculated using the EU method per Annex IV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/1773 and verified by an accredited third party. Without this data, your EU importer defaults to country-specific default values — which include a punitive markup.
en:What are the official default values: What are the official default values for India for 2026?
en:The country-specific default values f: The country-specific default values for the 2026 definitive phase are established in Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621 (16 December 2025). These are different from the world-average values in the transitional period document (Dec 2023) — they are set at the average emission intensity of India for each product and production route, plus the punitive markup (+10% in 2026). The transitional-period world average values from JRC study JRC134682 serve as the publicly available reference baseline used in our calculator. For exact country-specific 2026 default values for your specific products and CN codes, SustainCore conducts a certified assessment using the official implementing regulation data.
en:Can voluntary carbon credits reduce m: Can voluntary carbon credits reduce my CBAM liability?
en:No. Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2023: No. Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2023/956 is explicit: only an explicit carbon price effectively paid in the country of production — such as a national ETS or a carbon tax — can be deducted from CBAM certificate obligations. Voluntary carbon credits purchased by the supplier or the importer do not count. This is a hard legal rule, not an interpretation. India's emerging Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation Act, once fully operational with explicit per-tonne carbon price mechanisms, may create deductible costs in future — but this requires regulatory confirmation per the delegated acts on Art. 9 implementation expected in 2026.
en:I export less than 50 tonnes per year: I export less than 50 tonnes per year. Am I exempt?
en:The 50-tonne de minimis threshold per: The 50-tonne de minimis threshold per Article 2(3a) of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083 is assessed at the EU importer level — covering their total annual imports of cement, iron and steel, aluminium and fertilisers from all origins combined. If your EU importer purchases CBAM goods from multiple suppliers, their total may exceed 50 tonnes even if your individual portion is small. The threshold does not apply per-supplier or per-shipment. Hydrogen and electricity are excluded from the de minimis rule regardless of volume. Confirm your EU importer's total CBAM import volume to determine whether the threshold applies to them.
en:Why do fertilisers receive a lower de: Why do fertilisers receive a lower default value markup than other sectors?
en:The European Commission CBAM Committe: The European Commission CBAM Committee adopted a special treatment for fertilisers in December 2025 (confirmed in Implementing Regulation EU 2025/2621): while steel, aluminium and cement default values carry +10% markup in 2026, rising to +30% by 2028, fertiliser default values carry only a +1% markup across all years. This reflects the fertiliser industry's concern that high CBAM costs would be passed through to consumer food prices — an inflation and food security consideration that led the Commission to constrain the fertiliser markup. As a result, the incentive to report actual verified emissions data is proportionally smaller for fertilisers than for steel or aluminium.
en:What happens to CBAM costs as the pha: What happens to CBAM costs as the phase-in factor increases towards 100%?
en:The CBAM phase-in factor reflects the: The CBAM phase-in factor reflects the progressive phase-out of free ETS allowances. In 2026 at 2.5%, CBAM certificates represent only 2.5% of total embedded emissions × ETS price. By 2034 at 100%, the full embedded emissions carry the full ETS price. Simultaneously, the EU ETS price is projected to rise from ~€70 today to ~€130 by 2030 as allowances tighten. The combined effect: a 10,000-tonne steel exporter with 1.9 tCO₂/t faces ~€33,250 in CBAM costs in 2026 (2.5% × €70) versus ~€2.47 million in 2034 (100% × €130). This 74× increase in cost over 8 years is the strongest possible incentive to start reducing embedded emissions now.

en:For most manufacturing organisations,: For most manufacturing organisations, the timeline from project kickoff to certification is 4-6 months. This depends on organisational size, complexity, existing documentation maturity, and resource availability. Organisations with existing management systems (ISO 9001, for example) typically achieve certification faster due to familiarity with documentation requirements and audit processes. We provide a realistic timeline during the gap analysis phase based on your specific situation.
en:What is the difference between ISO 14: What is the difference between ISO 14001:2015 and earlier versions?
en:ISO 14001:2015 introduced several sig: ISO 14001:2015 introduced several significant changes: adoption of Annex SL high-level structure (facilitating integration with other ISO management systems), explicit requirement for leadership commitment, risk-based thinking replacing preventive action, lifecycle perspective for environmental aspects, and strengthened requirements for communication and documented information. The 2015 version places greater emphasis on environmental performance and strategic alignment with organisational objectives rather than just documentation compliance.
en:Can we integrate ISO 14001 with ISO 9: Can we integrate ISO 14001 with ISO 9001 and ISO 45001?
en:Yes — all three standards use the Ann: Yes — all three standards use the Annex SL structure, making integration straightforward. Common elements include context of the organisation, leadership, planning, support, performance evaluation, and improvement. Integrated management systems (IMS) reduce documentation redundancy, simplify audit processes, and create operational efficiencies. We design EMS documentation to align with your existing QMS structure, enabling seamless integration and combined certification audits that reduce audit time and cost.
en:What are the ongoing requirements aft: What are the ongoing requirements after certification?
en:ISO 14001 certification follows a 3-y: ISO 14001 certification follows a 3-year cycle. After initial certification, surveillance audits are conducted annually (typically covering a portion of the standard each time). A full recertification audit occurs before the certificate expires. Ongoing requirements include maintaining the EMS, conducting internal audits, holding management reviews, tracking environmental objectives, and implementing continual improvement. We offer post-certification support packages covering surveillance audit preparation and system maintenance.
en:How does ISO 14001 help with EcoVadis: How does ISO 14001 help with EcoVadis scoring?
en:ISO 14001 certification directly stre: ISO 14001 certification directly strengthens your EcoVadis Environment theme score. The certificate itself is weighted evidence of a mature environmental management system. Beyond the certificate, ISO 14001 generates the policies, procedures, KPIs, targets, and performance data that EcoVadis assessors look for when scoring the Environment theme. Organisations with ISO 14001 typically score significantly higher on Environment compared to those without — often the difference between Bronze and Silver, or Silver and Gold medal status.

en:ISO 45001:2018 replaced OHSAS 18001 w: ISO 45001:2018 replaced OHSAS 18001 with several significant changes: adoption of Annex SL structure for integration with other ISO standards, explicit requirement for top management leadership and accountability, emphasis on worker participation and consultation, risk-based thinking approach, requirements for managing risks and opportunities (not just hazards), and focus on organisational context. OHSAS 18001 certificates expired in March 2021 — organisations must now certify to ISO 45001.

en:For most organisations, the timeline: For most organisations, the timeline from project kickoff to certification is 4-6 months. High-hazard operations requiring extensive HIRA development may require additional time.
en:What is the worker participation requ: What is the worker participation requirement?
en:ISO 45001 requires organisations to e: ISO 45001 requires organisations to establish processes for consultation and participation of workers at all levels. This includes involvement in hazard identification and risk assessment, determination of controls, incident investigation, development of OH&S policy and objectives, and decisions about monitoring and measurement. Worker participation mechanisms must be documented and workers must have access to relevant OH&S information.

We design OHSMS documentation to align with existing EMS structure, enabling combined audits and reducing overall management system burden.

en:ISO 45001 certification is one of the: ISO 45001 certification is one of the most efficient ways to improve your EcoVadis Labour & Human Rights theme score. en:Most organisations achieve 10-30% ene: Most organisations achieve 10-30% energy cost reduction within the first 2-3 years of ISO 50001 implementation. en:For designated consumers under India': For designated consumers under India's PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) scheme, ISO 50001 provides the management system framework to achieve specific energy consumption (SEC) targets. Energy is often a significant environmental aspect, so integration creates natural alignment. We design EnMS documentation to integrate with existing EMS structure, enabling combined audits that reduce audit burden and cost.
en:What is an Energy Performance Indicat: What is an Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)?
en:An EnPI is a quantitative value or me: An EnPI is a quantitative value or measure of energy performance — essentially a metric that tracks how efficiently your organisation uses energy. EnPIs can be absolute (total energy consumption), relative (energy per unit of production), or statistical (regression-based models accounting for multiple variables). The key is selecting EnPIs that are meaningful for your significant energy uses, can be influenced by operational decisions, and account for relevant variables like production volume, weather, or operating hours.

en:Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) is the legislative mandate that holds Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) accountable for the end-of-life management of their products.

en:Non-fulfillment of EPR targets trigge: Non-fulfillment of EPR targets triggers "Environmental Compensation" (EC) based on the quantity of shortfall. Compensation is penal and does not absolve the liability.
en:The CPCB portal automatically calcula: The CPCB portal automatically calculates targets based on your sales data. Any unfulfilled balance at the end of the financial year is automatically flagged for compensation assessments.
en:Paying Environmental Compensation doe: Paying Environmental Compensation does not reset your target. The shortfall is carried forward to the next year, creating a compounding liability if not managed strategically.
en:Persistent non-filing of annual retur: Persistent non-filing of annual returns or non-fulfillment of targets can lead to suspension of PIBO registration, effectively halting your legal right to import or produce.

en:The Hazardous & Other Wastes (Managem: The Hazardous & Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016, mandate that every industry generating any hazardous waste must obtain formal authorization from the State Pollution Control Board.
en:We provide the full technical pathway: We provide the full technical pathway to secure authorization, including waste characterization, Rule 9 documentation, and TSDF coordination.
en:Authorisation starts with correct cha: Authorisation starts with correct characterization of the waste streams generating from your processes, as defined under Schedule 1 and Schedule 2 of the Rules.
en:A process-based list (e.g., spent sol: A process-based list (e.g., spent solvents from labs, ETP sludge, used oil) that automatically classifies a waste stream as "hazardous" if it matches the process.
en:Determined by technical chemical anal: Determined by technical chemical analysis. If specific contaminants (like heavy metals) exceed the set mg/kg threshold, the waste must be managed as hazardous.
en:Special permission required when an i: Special permission required when an industry wants to "reuse" or "utilize" hazardous waste from another process as a resource, bypassing landfill disposal.
en:The MoEF&CC and CPCB have established: The MoEF&CC and CPCB have established strict "Environmental Compensation" regimes specifically for the improper handling of hazardous wastes.
en:Every movement of hazardous waste mus: Every movement of hazardous waste must be tracked via a digital (online SPCB portal) or physical Manifest (Form 10). Missing or fake manifests trigger immediate show-cause notices.
en:Industries are permitted to store haz: Industries are permitted to store hazardous waste for a maximum of 90 days. Exceeding this period without board permission results in penalties and CTO violation.
en:Dumping of hazardous waste in non-aut: Dumping of hazardous waste in non-authorized landfills or selling it to non-authorized recyclers is a major violation that can lead to permanent facility closure.

en:India's environmental regulatory fram: India's environmental regulatory framework has expanded significantly — with overlapping central and state requirements, NGT directions, and sector-specific rules creating a complex compliance landscape that demands systematic management.
en:Environmental non-compliance carries: Environmental non-compliance carries severe consequences beyond financial penalties — including operational disruption, personal liability, and reputational damage.
en:The Environment Protection Act, Water: The Environment Protection Act, Water Act, and Air Act prescribe penalties up to ₹1 crore for violations, with enhanced penalties for continuing offences. NGT has imposed penalties exceeding ₹100 crore in environmental compensation cases.
en:SPCBs have authority to issue closure: SPCBs have authority to issue closure directions for non-compliance with consent conditions or emission/effluent standards. Closure orders cause immediate operational disruption, supply chain failures, and significant revenue loss.
en:Environmental laws impose personal li: Environmental laws impose personal liability on directors and officers. Under Section 15 of the Environment Protection Act, individuals in charge can face imprisonment up to 5 years for violations. Directors cannot hide behind corporate structure.
en:Environmental violations can trigger: Environmental violations can trigger loan covenant breaches, increase insurance premiums, and complicate refinancing. Banks conducting ESG due diligence increasingly flag compliance issues as credit risks.
en:Global buyers conducting supply chain: Global buyers conducting supply chain due diligence will not source from facilities with compliance violations. A single NGT order or closure notice can result in immediate delisting from approved vendor lists.
en:Environmental liabilities can derail: Environmental liabilities can derail M&A transactions, reduce valuations, or require extensive indemnities. Buyers conduct detailed environmental due diligence — undisclosed compliance gaps create material transaction risk.

Trigger events for additional audits include regulatory changes, expansion projects, M&A activity, or show-cause notices.

en:Industrial facilities are legally res: Industrial facilities are legally responsible for protecting groundwater resources. CGWA NOC conditions, Environmental Clearance stipulations, and SPCB requirements mandate monitoring. Beyond compliance, contamination creates massive liability exposure.
en:Groundwater contamination carries sev: Groundwater contamination carries severe consequences — legal, financial, operational, and reputational.
en:The National Green Tribunal has impos: The National Green Tribunal has imposed environmental compensation exceeding ₹100 crore for groundwater contamination cases. NGT orders have mandated facility closure, remediation at company cost, and compensation to affected communities.
en:Central Ground Water Authority NOC co: Central Ground Water Authority NOC conditions require periodic groundwater monitoring and reporting. Failure to comply can result in NOC revocation, extraction restrictions, and penalty for illegal groundwater use.
en:Groundwater contamination affecting d: Groundwater contamination affecting drinking water sources creates intense community opposition. Contamination allegations — even unproven — damage reputation, invite regulatory scrutiny, and threaten social license to operate.
en:Groundwater remediation is technicall: Groundwater remediation is technically complex and extremely expensive — often running into crores for contaminated aquifers. Prevention through monitoring is far more cost-effective than remediation after contamination occurs.

en:Monitoring frequency depends on regul: Monitoring frequency depends on regulatory requirements and site risk profile. CGWA NOC and EC conditions typically specify quarterly or half-yearly monitoring. For high-risk facilities (hazardous waste storage, chemical manufacturing) or sites with known contamination concerns, monthly monitoring may be appropriate. Seasonal monitoring (pre-monsoon, post-monsoon) captures aquifer recharge effects. We recommend at minimum quarterly monitoring for most industrial facilities.
en:Monitoring network design depends on: Monitoring network design depends on facility size, potential contamination sources, and hydrogeology. At minimum, you need one upgradient (background) well and two or more downgradient wells to detect contamination migration. Additional wells may be needed around specific sources (hazardous waste storage, ETP, chemical storage). CGWA or EC conditions may specify minimum requirements. We conduct hydrogeological assessment to design an adequate network.
en:Existing borewells can be used if the: Existing borewells can be used if they meet certain criteria — known construction details, appropriate depth and screen interval for the target aquifer, and suitable location relative to contamination sources. Production wells actively used for extraction are not ideal for monitoring due to mixing of water from different depths. Purpose-built monitoring wells with known construction provide more reliable data. We assess existing wells and recommend whether dedicated monitoring wells are needed.
en:If monitoring detects contamination a: If monitoring detects contamination above drinking water standards, we immediately notify you and conduct preliminary source assessment. Depending on severity, recommended actions may include: increased monitoring frequency, expanded monitoring network, source investigation, interim containment measures, or formal contamination assessment and remediation planning. Early detection through regular monitoring enables proactive response before contamination spreads or triggers regulatory action.
en:CDP Water questionnaire asks about wa: CDP Water questionnaire asks about water quality impacts and groundwater management practices. Groundwater monitoring data demonstrates responsible stewardship and provides evidence for responses about water quality management, pollution prevention, and environmental impact monitoring. Organisations with documented groundwater monitoring programmes score higher on water stewardship criteria. The trend data also supports target-setting for water quality improvement.

en:Comprehensive audit covering groundwa: Comprehensive audit covering groundwater extraction permits and monitoring compliance.
en:Ambient air quality monitoring measur: Ambient air quality monitoring measures pollutant concentrations in the outdoor air around your facility boundary and in surrounding communities — comparing them against India’s National Ambient Air Quality (NAAQ) Standards.
en:It is mandatory for Environmental Cle: It is mandatory for Environmental Clearance applications, EC compliance reporting, EIA studies and GPCB Consent to Operate conditions.
en:We provide NABL-accredited ambient ai: We provide NABL-accredited ambient air monitoring across seasonal baseline and continuous regulatory compliance programmes.
en:The Ministry of Environment, Forest a: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQ Standards) in 2009 under the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act. These are the legal benchmarks against which all ambient monitoring data is assessed.
en:We deploy the appropriate monitoring: We deploy the appropriate monitoring technology for each programme type — from portable Hi-Vol samplers for EIA baseline studies to online AAQMS stations for continuous real-time EC compliance monitoring.
en:Standard 24-hour filter-based samplin: Standard 24-hour filter-based sampling for PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, CO, heavy metals and other parameters at up to 8 simultaneous locations. Portable, rapid deployment, no infrastructure required.
en:Fixed or semi-portable automated stat: Fixed or semi-portable automated station providing real-time, continuous monitoring of PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃ and CO with GSM/internet data transmission. Required for EC compliance continuous monitoring conditions.
en:Low-cost passive diffusion tubes for: Low-cost passive diffusion tubes for SO₂, NO₂, NH₃, H₂S and VOC monitoring over 1–4 week exposure periods. Ideal for wide-area community impact screening and supplementary spatial coverage around Hi-Vol networks.
en:Environmental Clearances, half-yearly: Environmental Clearances, half-yearly compliance reports, EIA studies and GPCB inspections all require valid ambient air quality data. Without it, your regulatory submissions are incomplete and rejectable.
en:Every Environmental Impact Assessment: Every Environmental Impact Assessment study requires a minimum one-season (preferably two-season) baseline ambient air quality dataset from the study area — covering a 5–10 km radius around the proposed project. This baseline is mandatory for MoEF&CC / SEIAA EC application and TOR (Terms of Reference) compliance. Without it, your EIA report is technically deficient and will be rejected at scoping.
en:Industries holding Environmental Clea: Industries holding Environmental Clearances under the EIA Notification are required to submit half-yearly compliance reports to the MoEF&CC and SEIAA. These reports must include current ambient air quality monitoring data from the prescribed monitoring locations around the facility — typically the fence line, leeward side, nearest habitation and a background location.
en:GPCB Consent to Operate conditions fo: GPCB Consent to Operate conditions for Red and Orange category industries frequently require periodic ambient air quality monitoring at specified boundary locations, submitted as part of the annual or bi-annual consent renewal package. Gaps in monitoring data are treated as consent violations during GPCB inspection, with consequences for consent renewal and category classification.
en:Air dispersion modelling &mdash; requ: Air dispersion modelling — required in EIA studies to predict ground-level concentrations from stack emissions — must be validated against measured ambient air quality data. Without measured baseline and downwind data, the modelling is unvalidated and vulnerable to challenge during public consultation and EAC review hearings.
en:We design, conduct and report ambient: We design, conduct and report ambient air quality monitoring programmes covering EIA baseline studies, EC compliance monitoring, seasonal surveys and continuous AAQMS deployments — all producing NABL-accredited results in CPCB/GPCB-prescribed formats.
en:CPCB guideline-compliant monitoring l: CPCB guideline-compliant monitoring location selection considering prevailing wind direction, sensitive receptors (hospitals, schools, residential areas), topography and EC/GPCB prescribed locations. We optimise the network to provide maximum regulatory utility from minimum station count.
en:24-hour Hi-Vol sampling for PM₁₀, PM₂: 24-hour Hi-Vol sampling for PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, CO and additional parameters (Pb, HF, HCl, NH₃, VOCs as applicable) at all designated locations. Minimum 8 consecutive 24-hour sampling days per season per CPCB protocol for EIA baseline studies.
en:All collected filter samples analysed: All collected filter samples analysed at our NABL-accredited laboratory using gravimetric and wet chemical methods prescribed in IS 5182 and CPCB technical guidelines. Full QA/QC including field blanks, duplicates and spike recovery documentation.
en:Simultaneous measurement of wind spee: Simultaneous measurement of wind speed, wind direction, temperature, relative humidity and atmospheric stability class at each monitoring location — essential for air dispersion modelling validation and regulatory reporting.
en:Complete ambient air quality monitori: Complete ambient air quality monitoring report in CPCB/GPCB/MoEF prescribed format: all monitoring results vs. NAAQ Standards, wind roses, meteorological summaries, NABL test reports and station location maps. Ready for direct insertion into EIA chapters or EC compliance reports.
en:A five-stage process delivering a com: A five-stage process delivering a complete, submission-ready ambient air quality monitoring report within 15–20 working days of sampling completion.
en:Study area mapping, wind rose analysi: Study area mapping, wind rose analysis, sensitive receptor identification, monitoring location finalisation and logistics planning per CPCB guidelines and EC/TOR conditions.
en:Hi-Vol sampler and meteorological ins: Hi-Vol sampler and meteorological instrument installation at all designated locations. Equipment calibration verification, site security arrangements and sampling schedule commencement.
en:24-hour sampling cycles for minimum 8: 24-hour sampling cycles for minimum 8 days per location per season. Daily field inspection, filter and impinger changes, field data recording and chain-of-custody documentation.
en:NABL-accredited analysis of all sampl: NABL-accredited analysis of all samples using IS 5182 methods. Internal QA/QC, outlier review and preliminary data validation before report generation.
en:Complete monitoring report in prescri: Complete monitoring report in prescribed format with all results, NAAQ comparison, wind roses, meteorological data and NABL certificates. Direct submission to MoEF, SEIAA or GPCB as required.
en:Signed NABL laboratory analysis repor: Signed NABL laboratory analysis reports for all monitored parameters at all locations, accepted by MoEF&CC, SEIAA, GPCB, CPCB, courts and environmental tribunals.
en:Complete report in CPCB/GPCB prescrib: Complete report in CPCB/GPCB prescribed format: all measured concentrations tabulated against NAAQ Standards, summary statistics (mean, maximum, 98th percentile), compliance status and monitoring location maps.
en:Seasonal wind rose diagrams, meteorol: Seasonal wind rose diagrams, meteorological parameter tables (wind speed, direction, temperature, humidity, stability class) in the format required for air dispersion modelling and EIA chapter inclusion.
en:GIS-referenced site maps showing all: GIS-referenced site maps showing all monitoring station locations, study area boundary, sensitive receptors, wind direction overlays and land use classification — formatted for EIA chapter and regulatory submission.
en:For EIA engagement clients, a fully d: For EIA engagement clients, a fully drafted air environment baseline chapter incorporating monitoring data, analysis, NAAQ comparison, trend discussion and predictive modelling input data — ready for inclusion in your EIA report.
en:For EC compliance reporting, a ready-: For EC compliance reporting, a ready-to-submit data package covering all ambient monitoring results for the reporting period, formatted for the MoEF&CC online compliance reporting portal and SEIAA submission.
en:Industry responding to CPCB / GPCB no: Industry responding to CPCB / GPCB notice for ambient monitoring data
en:Our ambient monitoring complies with: Our ambient monitoring complies with the full hierarchy of Indian environmental law and CPCB technical standards — producing data accepted by every regulatory authority, tribunal and court in India.
en:How many monitoring locations and sam: How many monitoring locations and sampling days are required for an EIA baseline?
en:CPCB guidelines for EIA studies typic: CPCB guidelines for EIA studies typically require a minimum of 4 monitoring locations within the 5 km study area: one upwind (background), one leeward (downwind, most impacted direction), one nearest sensitive receptor (school, hospital, habitation) and one fence line location. The minimum monitoring duration is 8 consecutive 24-hour sampling days per season per location. Most EIA TOR conditions require two-season baseline (typically pre-monsoon and post-monsoon), making the total dataset 16 sampling days per location across two seasons. The number of locations can increase for larger study areas or projects with multiple emission sources — we advise on the optimal network during the scoping phase.
en:The parameters required for EC compli: The parameters required for EC compliance ambient monitoring are specified in your specific Environmental Clearance conditions and vary by industry category. The minimum standard set for most industries includes PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂ and NO₂. Industries with specific additional pollutant risks (e.g., cement: fluoride; chemicals: HCl, NH₃, specific VOCs; lead smelters: Pb) will have additional parameters listed in their EC. We review your EC conditions at kickoff to confirm the exact parameter list, ensure all are covered and identify any parameters requiring specialised analytical methods not available in standard Hi-Vol sampling — which require additional planning.
en:Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monito: Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (AAQMS) are increasingly accepted by CPCB and some SPCBs as an alternative or supplement to periodic Hi-Vol sampling for EC compliance reporting — particularly for PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃ and CO. However, they cannot replace NABL-accredited Hi-Vol analysis for all parameters specified in an EC condition, and some parameters (heavy metals, fluoride, specific VOCs) still require Hi-Vol filter collection and laboratory analysis. We advise on the most efficient combination of AAQMS continuous monitoring and targeted periodic Hi-Vol sampling to satisfy all EC conditions at minimum total cost.
en:Can the same monitoring data be used: Can the same monitoring data be used for both EIA and GPCB compliance?
en:Yes &mdash; provided the monitoring n: Yes — provided the monitoring network, parameters, sampling duration and NABL accreditation meet the requirements of both purposes. EIA baseline studies and GPCB compliance monitoring share the same NAAQ Standard benchmarks and CPCB sampling protocols. We design monitoring programmes to simultaneously satisfy EIA TOR conditions, EC compliance requirements and GPCB consent conditions wherever possible — avoiding the cost and complexity of running separate parallel programmes. The key is confirming the specific locations and parameters required by each authority before the sampling campaign begins, so that a single well-designed campaign generates regulatory-grade data acceptable to all.
en:Ambient air quality baseline data is: Ambient air quality baseline data is mandatory for every EC application and half-yearly compliance report. We provide both the monitoring and the EC advisory in one integrated programme.
en:EIA studies require a seasonal ambien: EIA studies require a seasonal ambient air quality baseline covering the full study area. We conduct the monitoring and prepare the air environment chapter.
en:Ambient and stack monitoring together: Ambient and stack monitoring together provide the complete regulatory air quality picture — source emissions and community-level receptor impact.
en:CEMS &mdash; Continuous Emission Moni: CEMS — Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems — are automated systems that measure and transmit real-time stack emission data 24×7 to the CPCB’s central data server. They are
en:mandatory for 17 categories of large: mandatory for 17 categories of large industries under CPCB’s 2014 Direction and subsequent amendments.
en:Non-compliant industries face automat: Non-compliant industries face automatic flagging, penalties and closure notices. We provide end-to-end CEMS services: system selection, installation, calibration, CPCB connectivity, AMC and regulatory compliance management.
en:Industry sectors mandated for CEMS by: Industry sectors mandated for CEMS by CPCB
en:Continuous data transmission to CPCB: Continuous data transmission to CPCB server
en:A complete CEMS comprises four interc: A complete CEMS comprises four interconnected subsystems that together provide legally compliant, continuous, tamper-evident emission data transmitted directly to the CPCB central server.
en:Extractive probe inserted into the st: Extractive probe inserted into the stack draws a representative gas sample. Sample conditioning system removes moisture and particulates before analysis.
en:Certified gas analysers measure SO₂,: Certified gas analysers measure SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, O₂ and particulates in real time. Must be QAL1-certified under EN 14181 / CPCB specifications.
en:DAS collects, timestamps and stores a: DAS collects, timestamps and stores all analyser outputs. Calculates 15-minute averages, daily averages and emission loads. Includes tamper-evident logging.
en:Secure, encrypted data transmission t: Secure, encrypted data transmission to the CPCB central data server every 15 minutes. GPRS/internet connectivity with automatic retransmission on failure.
en:The CPCB has mandated CEMS for 17 maj: The CPCB has mandated CEMS for 17 major polluting industry categories under its 2014 Direction. The following industries must have operational CEMS transmitting data to the CPCB server.
en:The CPCB monitors data transmission i: The CPCB monitors data transmission in real time. Gaps in data, sensor failures or CEMS downtime of more than 10% in any month automatically trigger non-compliance flags — regardless of actual emission levels.
en:The CPCB&rsquo;s central server monit: The CPCB’s central server monitors data continuity in real time. Any gap exceeding 15 minutes, data transmission failure, or sensor malfunction is automatically flagged. Industries with more than 10% data unavailability in a month are listed as non-compliant — without any inspector visit.
en:Functional CEMS with active data tran: Functional CEMS with active data transmission is a mandatory condition in the CTO of all mandated industries. An inoperative or non-transmitting CEMS is treated as a violation of CTO conditions and can result in notice of consent revocation, irrespective of whether actual emissions are within limits.
en:When CEMS data exceeds prescribed lim: When CEMS data exceeds prescribed limits for two or more consecutive 15-minute averaging periods, the CPCB server automatically generates an exceedance alert. This triggers regulatory correspondence and, in severe cases, immediate show-cause action without waiting for periodic manual monitoring reports.
en:We provide the full spectrum of CEMS: We provide the full spectrum of CEMS services: system design, equipment supply, installation, CPCB connectivity commissioning, calibration, annual maintenance and regulatory data management.
en:Selection of QAL1-certified analysers: Selection of QAL1-certified analysers and sampling systems appropriate for your stack conditions (temperature, flow, gas matrix). System design for single or multi-stack installations, including shelter, conditioning and DAS specifications.
en:Physical installation of sampling pro: Physical installation of sampling probes, conditioning systems, analysers and DAS. Site acceptance testing, zero and span calibration using certified reference gases, and initial 30-day performance demonstration before CPCB connection.
en:Configuration of secure GPRS/internet: Configuration of secure GPRS/internet data transmission to the CPCB central server in the required XML/JSON format. Connectivity testing, data validation and generation of the CPCB-required installation certificate and connectivity report.
en:EN 14181 QAL2 calibration using paral: EN 14181 QAL2 calibration using parallel reference method testing to establish system-specific calibration functions. Annual System Test (AST) with parallel reference method comparison, as required for continued CPCB compliance of the installed system.
en:Scheduled preventive maintenance, con: Scheduled preventive maintenance, consumables replacement (filters, probes, reference gas cylinders), corrective maintenance for analyser failures, data gap management and monthly data availability reports — maintaining minimum 90% CPCB data availability.
en:A six-stage implementation programme: A six-stage implementation programme from site survey to live CPCB data transmission, typically completed in 8–14 weeks depending on site complexity and number of stacks.
en:Stack characterisation, access assess: Stack characterisation, access assessment, gas matrix analysis and CPCB parameter list confirmation against your industry category.
en:Analyser selection (QAL1 certified),: Analyser selection (QAL1 certified), sampling and conditioning system design, DAS specification and site layout drawing for SPCB approval.
en:Mechanical installation of probes, co: Mechanical installation of probes, conditioning, analysers, DAS shelter. Electrical and instrument loop checks. Safety testing.
en:Zero/span calibration, linearity chec: Zero/span calibration, linearity checks, response time testing and 30-day reliability demonstration per CPCB commissioning protocol.
en:Data transmission configuration, CPCB: Data transmission configuration, CPCB server registration, connectivity test report and installation certificate preparation for regulatory submission.
en:Ongoing scheduled maintenance, QAL2 /: Ongoing scheduled maintenance, QAL2 / AST calibration, data gap management and monthly availability reports to maintain CPCB compliance.
en:Fully operational CEMS transmitting d: Fully operational CEMS transmitting data live to the CPCB central server — meeting all CPCB technical specifications and QAL1 analyser certification requirements.
en:Formal CPCB installation and connecti: Formal CPCB installation and connectivity certificate confirming system compliance — required for submission to your SPCB as part of CTO renewal documentation.
en:EN 14181 QAL2 calibration report with: EN 14181 QAL2 calibration report with system calibration functions, reference method parallel test data and uncertainty analysis — required for SPCB annual submission.
en:Monthly report showing data availabil: Monthly report showing data availability percentage, exceedance events, maintenance interventions and overall system performance against the 90% CPCB availability requirement.
en:Annual parallel reference method test: Annual parallel reference method test confirming CEMS accuracy is maintained within specified tolerance limits — required annually under EN 14181 / CPCB guidelines.
en:Real-time SMS/email alerts to your EH: Real-time SMS/email alerts to your EHS team when CEMS data approaches or exceeds limits, allowing corrective action before CPCB automatic enforcement flags are triggered.
en:Fertiliser / petrochemical plant in C: Fertiliser / petrochemical plant in CPCB Direction list
en:CEMS is mandated by CPCB for 17 major: CEMS is mandated by CPCB for 17 major industry categories under its 2014 Direction and subsequent amendments. The mandated categories include: thermal power plants, cement plants, integrated iron and steel, petroleum refineries, fertiliser plants, pulp and paper mills, sugar industries, distilleries, chlor-alkali plants, caustic soda plants, common effluent treatment plants (CETPs), common biomedical waste treatment facilities, common hazardous waste incineration and treatment facilities, glass manufacturing, tanneries, zinc and lead smelters, and copper smelters. The specific stacks, parameters and transmission requirements vary by sector. You should also check whether your SPCB or individual CTO specifies CEMS requirements beyond the national mandate, as several states have expanded the list.
en:The CPCB server automatically records: The CPCB server automatically records data gaps. If transmission is interrupted for more than 15 minutes, the gap is logged. If total data unavailability exceeds 10% in any calendar month, the industry is automatically classified as non-compliant for that month — regardless of actual emission levels. Persistent non-compliance triggers a show-cause notice. Under our AMC, we monitor transmission health 24×7 and dispatch engineers within 4 hours of any system fault to restore connectivity and minimise data gaps. We also maintain substitute measurement data using parallel portable analysers for periods where the CEMS is unavailable for maintenance, per CPCB data substitution guidelines.
en:These are three quality assurance lev: These are three quality assurance levels defined under European Standard EN 14181, which the CPCB has adopted as the reference for CEMS quality assurance in India. QAL1 is the type-approval certification of the analyser equipment itself — demonstrated by the manufacturer before sale. Only QAL1-certified analysers are accepted for CPCB-mandated CEMS. QAL2 is the site-specific calibration of a QAL1-certified analyser installed at your particular stack — conducted after installation using parallel reference method measurements. It establishes site-specific calibration functions accounting for your gas matrix. AST (Annual Surveillance Test) is a repeat parallel reference method test conducted annually to confirm that the QAL2 calibration remains valid. All three are required for a CPCB-compliant CEMS installation.
en:Manual isokinetic stack monitoring pr: Manual isokinetic stack monitoring provides the reference method data required for QAL2 and AST calibration of your installed CEMS.
en:Ambient monitoring around your facili: Ambient monitoring around your facility boundary provides the receptor-side data that contextualises CEMS emission measurements for regulatory and community purposes.
en:CEMS installation and data availabili: CEMS installation and data availability are mandatory compliance conditions in Environmental Clearances for large industries under the EIA Notification.
en:Stack emission monitoring is the lega: Stack emission monitoring is the legally mandated measurement of pollutants discharged from industrial chimneys and flue gas stacks — required by the CPCB, SPCB and MoEF&CC under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 and Water & Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Acts.
en:We conduct NABL-accredited stack moni: We conduct NABL-accredited stack monitoring across all industrial sectors with full regulatory report submission.
en:We measure all parameters specified b: We measure all parameters specified by CPCB General Standards, GPCB / SPCB sector-specific standards and MoEF&CC Environment (Protection) Rules schedules applicable to your industry.
en:Every industry with a chimney, boiler: Every industry with a chimney, boiler, DG set, kiln or furnace above the threshold capacity is legally required to conduct periodic stack emission monitoring. Non-compliance is a criminal offence under Indian environmental law.
en:Stack monitoring is mandated under th: Stack monitoring is mandated under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. The CPCB and respective SPCBs (GPCB for Gujarat) prescribe monitoring frequency, parameters and reporting formats — typically quarterly or half-yearly for most industries.
en:Stack monitoring reports are a mandat: Stack monitoring reports are a mandatory condition of every Consent to Operate issued by the SPCB. Failure to submit monitoring data on time is treated as a violation of CTO conditions and can trigger suspension or revocation of the consent — shutting operations.
en:The CPCB and most SPCBs require that: The CPCB and most SPCBs require that stack emission monitoring be conducted only by NABL-accredited laboratories. Reports from non-accredited sources are rejected for regulatory submission and do not provide legal protection in show-cause proceedings or judicial hearings.
en:The CPCB&rsquo;s Pollution Index syst: The CPCB’s Pollution Index system rates industries Red, Orange, Green or White based on emission levels and compliance history. Consistently high stack emissions push your index score upward, attracting more frequent inspections, public reporting exposure and mandatory pollution control investment orders.
en:We handle every stage of the stack mo: We handle every stage of the stack monitoring cycle: pre-sampling preparation, on-site isokinetic sampling and field measurement, NABL-accredited laboratory analysis, regulatory report preparation and SPCB/CPCB submission.
en:On-site inspection of all emission po: On-site inspection of all emission points, identification of stacks requiring monitoring under your CTO conditions, measurement port location review, and confirmation of sampling access arrangements before monitoring day.
en:Trained field engineers conduct isoki: Trained field engineers conduct isokinetic particulate sampling and simultaneous gas phase sampling using calibrated equipment — ensuring sampling is conducted at the correct velocity to capture representative particulate matter concentrations.
en:On-site real-time measurement of SO₂,: On-site real-time measurement of SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂ and O₂ using portable analysers calibrated against reference gases, with simultaneous measurement of flue gas temperature, velocity and moisture content per CPCB protocols.
en:Collected samples are analysed at our: Collected samples are analysed at our NABL-accredited laboratory using CPCB-prescribed analytical methods — producing results legally accepted by all SPCBs, the CPCB, courts and environmental tribunals.
en:Complete monitoring report prepared i: Complete monitoring report prepared in SPCB-prescribed format with all results, calculations, equipment calibration records, field data sheets and QA/QC documentation. We prepare your submission directly for GPCB or applicable SPCB portal upload.
en:A five-stage process from site prepar: A five-stage process from site preparation to regulatory submission, delivering legally accepted results within 5–7 working days of sampling.
en:Review of CTO conditions, identificat: Review of CTO conditions, identification of all emission points, parameter list confirmation, access and safety checks.
en:Isokinetic particulate sampling, in-s: Isokinetic particulate sampling, in-situ gas analysis, flue gas characterisation and simultaneous multi-point measurement at all stacks.
en:NABL-accredited analysis of collected: NABL-accredited analysis of collected samples using CPCB-prescribed methods. Internal QA/QC review and result validation.
en:Full monitoring report in SPCB format: Full monitoring report in SPCB format: all results vs. standards, emission load calculations, exceedance flags and corrective action recommendations.
en:SPCB portal submission, submission ac: SPCB portal submission, submission acknowledgement and post-report compliance advisory if any parameters exceed prescribed limits.
en:Signed and stamped test report from o: Signed and stamped test report from our NABL-accredited laboratory for all measured parameters, legally valid for regulatory submission to any SPCB, CPCB or tribunal.
en:Complete report in prescribed SPCB/CP: Complete report in prescribed SPCB/CPCB format including all emission concentrations, volumetric flow rates, emission loads (kg/hr) and comparison against applicable standards with compliance status.
en:Original signed field data sheets for: Original signed field data sheets for every stack, recording sampling conditions, equipment readings, field observations and chain-of-custody documentation for all collected samples.
en:Calibration certificates and records: Calibration certificates and records for all instruments used during sampling, required for SPCB data validation and environmental audit purposes.
en:For repeat clients, a trend analysis: For repeat clients, a trend analysis comparing current results against previous monitoring cycles — identifying improving or deteriorating emissions trends before they attract regulatory attention.
en:Written advisory for any parameters e: Written advisory for any parameters exceeding prescribed limits, covering probable causes, immediate corrective measures and timeline recommendations to achieve compliance before the next monitoring cycle.
en:Our stack monitoring covers the full: Our stack monitoring covers the full hierarchy of Indian environmental regulations — central, state and sector-specific — as well as international reference methods for technically complex parameters.
en:CPCB General Standards for Gaseous Em: CPCB General Standards for Gaseous Emissions
en:Any stack subject to CPCB/SPCB Consen: Any stack subject to CPCB/SPCB Consent to Operate conditions
en:Monitoring frequency depends on your: Monitoring frequency depends on your industry category and SPCB consent conditions. Red category industries are typically required to monitor quarterly; Orange category half-yearly; and Green category annually. However, your specific CTO conditions override these general timelines — some industries with high-impact stacks are required to monitor monthly. CEMS-fitted units have continuous data obligations. We review your CTO conditions at kickoff and establish the monitoring calendar to ensure you never miss a deadline.
en:Isokinetic sampling is the technique: Isokinetic sampling is the technique of drawing stack gas into a sampling nozzle at exactly the same velocity as the gas flowing in the duct — so that particles enter the probe without being deflected or concentrated by velocity differences. If sampling is not isokinetic (sub- or super-isokinetic), heavier particles are either over- or under-sampled, producing results that are not representative of actual emissions. Indian Standard IS 11255 and US EPA Method 5 both mandate isokinetic sampling for particulate matter measurement. Our engineers calculate and maintain isokinetic conditions in real time throughout each sampling run.
en:What happens if our stack emissions e: What happens if our stack emissions exceed CPCB limits?
en:If results exceed prescribed limits,: If results exceed prescribed limits, you are required to submit the report as measured — falsifying results is a criminal offence. The SPCB will typically issue a show-cause notice requiring you to explain the exceedance and submit a corrective action plan with a timeline for achieving compliance. In serious or repeat cases, the SPCB can issue a closure direction. We provide an immediate written compliance advisory identifying the most probable technical causes and recommending specific corrective actions — pollution control equipment upgrades, operational changes, fuel switching or stack parameter corrections — with timeframes to achieve compliance before the next monitoring cycle.
en:Yes &mdash; DG sets above 15 kVA capa: Yes — DG sets above 15 kVA capacity in Red and Orange category industries are subject to stack monitoring requirements and must comply with CPCB emission standards for DG sets (particulate matter, NOₓ and CO). Larger DG sets above 800 kVA may also be subject to SOₓ limits if running on high-sulphur fuel. Many companies overlook DG set monitoring, which then appears as a gap during SPCB inspections. We include all DG sets above applicable thresholds in our monitoring surveys.
en:Where CPCB mandates real-time CEMS, w: Where CPCB mandates real-time CEMS, we support installation, calibration, data transmission and CPCB portal connectivity.
en:Baseline and compliance ambient air q: Baseline and compliance ambient air quality monitoring around your facility boundary — required for EC conditions and EIA studies.
en:Stack monitoring data forms a critica: Stack monitoring data forms a critical input to Environmental Clearance applications and half-yearly compliance reports under EC conditions.
en:Workplace air quality monitoring meas: Workplace air quality monitoring measures the concentration of airborne chemical, dust and biological hazards at worker breathing zones inside your facility — comparing them against Occupational Exposure Limits (OELs).
en:It is a legal duty under the Factorie: It is a legal duty under the Factories Act, 1948, the Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemicals Rules and ISO 45001.
en:It also directly drives your EcoVadis: It also directly drives your EcoVadis Labour score and BRSR Principle 5 disclosures on worker health and safety.
en:We measure personal and area concentr: We measure personal and area concentrations against ACGIH Threshold Limit Values (TLVs), OSHA PELs and Indian Factory Schedule standards. Below are commonly monitored substances across industrial sectors.
en:A comprehensive workplace air quality: A comprehensive workplace air quality survey covers three distinct sampling zones, each answering a different question about worker exposure and emission source control.
en:Lapel or helmet-mounted personal air: Lapel or helmet-mounted personal air sampler worn by the worker for the full 8-hour shift. Provides the most legally defensible measure of individual exposure. Required for ACGIH TLV/TWA comparison and chronic health risk assessment.
en:Fixed samplers placed at representati: Fixed samplers placed at representative locations across the work area at breathing height (1.5m). Identifies spatial distribution of contamination and areas requiring engineering controls or improved ventilation.
en:Samplers positioned close to specific: Samplers positioned close to specific emission sources — open tanks, mixing operations, welding points, furnace openings — to characterise source strength for local exhaust ventilation design.
en:Background and control zone measureme: Background and control zone measurements confirming that engineering barriers are effective and that administrative and clerical workers near production areas are not at risk of secondary exposure.
en:Unmonitored workplace air quality exp: Unmonitored workplace air quality exposes your business to criminal liability, insurance exclusions, occupational disease compensation claims, ISO 45001 non-conformances and EcoVadis score penalties — simultaneously.
en:Under Sections 11&ndash;20 of the Fac: Under Sections 11–20 of the Factories Act, 1948, employers have a statutory duty to maintain workplace environments free from hazardous concentrations of dust, fumes and chemicals. Factory Inspectors have powers to enter, inspect and prosecute. Failure to monitor is itself an offence, independent of whether actual exposures exceed limits.
en:ISO 45001 Clause 6.1 requires hazard: ISO 45001 Clause 6.1 requires hazard identification and occupational health risk assessment as a core management system requirement. Workplace air quality monitoring data is the primary evidence base for chemical exposure risk assessment. Without it, an ISO 45001 audit will raise a major non-conformance.
en:EcoVadis evaluates occupational healt: EcoVadis evaluates occupational health and safety management as part of its Labour & Human Rights theme — one of the four weighted scoring criteria. Documented workplace air quality monitoring with corrective action programmes significantly improves your Labour theme score. BRSR Principle 5 (P5) requires specific KPI disclosure on workplace health and safety, including monitoring data.
en:Chronic occupational diseases &mdash;: Chronic occupational diseases — silicosis, asbestosis, chronic solvent encephalopathy, noise-induced hearing loss — attract workmen’s compensation claims, insurance litigation and reputational damage. Regular workplace monitoring, documented corrective actions and engineering controls are your primary legal defence against occupational disease compensation claims.
en:We conduct complete industrial hygien: We conduct complete industrial hygiene surveys: hazard identification, sampling strategy design, personal and area air sampling, NABL-accredited laboratory analysis and a full occupational exposure assessment report with risk-ranked corrective recommendations.
en:Industrial hygienist-led facility wal: Industrial hygienist-led facility walk-through to identify all chemical, dust and biological hazards, assess exposure routes, evaluate existing controls and design a targeted sampling strategy covering the most material exposure scenarios.
en:Full-shift (8-hour) personal air samp: Full-shift (8-hour) personal air sampling at the breathing zone of workers in high-exposure job categories, using calibrated low-flow pumps and appropriate sample media (XAD, OVS, silica gel, PTFE filters). Short-term samples for STEL assessment during peak-exposure tasks.
en:Fixed-location samplers across all pr: Fixed-location samplers across all production zones, storage areas, loading bays and control rooms — mapping the spatial distribution of contamination and identifying localised hotspots requiring priority engineering intervention.

en:The Time-Weighted Average (TWA) is th: The Time-Weighted Average (TWA) is the 8-hour average airborne concentration to which a worker may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects — the chronic exposure limit. The Short-Term Exposure Limit (STEL) is the 15-minute average concentration that should not be exceeded at any time during the working day, even if the 8-hour TWA is within limits — it protects against acute effects from brief high-concentration exposures. Both limits are set by ACGIH as Threshold Limit Values (TLVs), and where Indian standards exist under the Factories Act Schedules, those apply. We measure both — personal full-shift sampling for TWA and short-duration grab or pump samples during peak-exposure tasks for STEL.
en:Under ISO 45001 and good industrial h: Under ISO 45001 and good industrial hygiene practice, monitoring frequency depends on exposure risk level. Initial baseline monitoring should be conducted when any new chemical is introduced or processes change significantly. For ongoing surveillance: exposures below 0.1× OEL — annually or on process change; exposures 0.1–0.5× OEL — every 6 months; exposures 0.5–1× OEL — quarterly; exposures above OEL — monthly until corrective actions reduce concentrations below the limit. The Factories Act and specific industry rules (chemical, hazardous chemicals rules) may prescribe more frequent monitoring for specific substances or factory categories.
en:Occupational noise dosimetry and heat: Occupational noise dosimetry and heat stress measurement (WBGT — Wet Bulb Globe Temperature) are separate monitoring services from airborne chemical and dust monitoring, but we provide all three as part of a comprehensive industrial hygiene survey. Noise dosimetry measures workers’ daily noise exposure against the 85 dB(A) action level and 90 dB(A) limit under the Factories Act. WBGT heat stress monitoring is critical for workers in foundries, glass plants, steel mills and outdoor operations. We recommend combining air quality, noise and heat stress monitoring into a single facility survey to minimise production disruption and reduce total assessment cost.
